transcript.text: Hi!
Hi, Ben.
How are you?
Good, thank you.
Happy Wednesday.
Yes, indeed.
Where are you calling from?
An hour north of Boston, in a town called New Boston, New Hampshire.
Hmm.
I have never been there, but I'm in Rhode Island.
Hey, where?
Middletown, just north of Boston.
Yeah.
Very nice.
I went to URI for undergrad.
Of course.
Awesome.
Yes, and I spend my winter skiing at Cannon in New Hampshire, so there we go.
You asked me and you don't even know it.
I know.
Amazing.
Well, great to meet
You have chatted with Tucker so far.
Am I your next stop on this journey?
Round two.
Round two.
Cool.
So I can give you a bit of a sense of how we're thinking about this.
Lots is changing.
We just passed our one-year anniversary as a company.
So you might imagine that lots changes here.
So like something to keep in mind as you're thinking about this, but all for good reasons.
Um, so the account director is, um, really the strategic leader for kind of every account.
Um, and we are implementing right now, you know, we have been operating in a pod model and I think part of the org will continue to operate in a pod model, um, like classic agency setup.
The other, you know, we're also building, um, on the backend, our own.
you've and you
Platform that's generating content using AI workflows.

And so, you know, we're starting to think about the org structure with that direction in mind, thinking that eventually that product will become client facing and we will have both a services org and a more traditional kind of like product led org structure for clients that don't want to pay us 15k a month for services.
So, you know, thinking about that evolution, we're starting to implement a bit more of like a ticketing system for the way that directors actually operate.
So pods are going to be or are made up of an account strategist, or I guess an account manager is really what that person is going to do.
And more like traditional like CS kind of like project management, making sure that like deliverables are on track.
And then I'm.
Managing editor who is in those pipelines, like in the AI platform day to day.
So we're kind of like eating our own dog food, improving the product as we're generating deliverables.
So those teams will be like working with a stable set of clients and directors, you know, we're pretty engaged with our clients.
Right now we meet weekly.
And so, but, you know, that like strategic piece of a content audit, SEO strategy, like that doesn't need to change every week.
so we're thinking about optimizing the director's time for more of like a pooled basis of who needs what when and what are like the specific director skills.
Cause we have some directors that are like highly technical, super good, like really thinking a lot at the forefront of like AI and experimentation.
With like, LLM visibility, and then we have some directors who are like, have spent their whole careers in really traditional editorial and agency roles.
And, you know, some of our clients still are writing for human consumption, or want, like are reading every single article with a fine tooth comb.
so you want a director that's like thinking more about content strategy for that.
You know, we were assigning directors that way.

But I think like, given that we're also starting to position ourselves for more expansion of work streams, we want that flexibility of like, who's managing what part of an account, or setting strategy for what part of an account at a director level.
Wanted to give you that context, because it might be a little bit different than what Tucker said, because it's all happened in the last like week.
But, you know, the, like, we're looking for people that are incredibly, like curious about what's coming next, because
That's really the reason that we're hired.
You know, how do they optimize for this changing landscape for across AI and SEO?
And who are like excited about strategizing for content at scale?
And yeah, that is my little spiel.
Any questions?
Eventually, I mean, I do, but I feel like it's a little preliminary before I like I jump into anything that we may already just cover in our in our chat.
But thank you.
I trust me, one, two, three, four, etc.
But but with that being said, I mean, it sounds really interesting.
it sounds sincerely exactly where I am anyway, in my in my career, where I have been practicing SEO for almost.
VIZ of
10 years, a few years ago, Will Smith is hardly recognizable eating spaghetti, and now it could be done.
And it's like, holy cow, this is absolutely incredible.
Generative AI comes out, and then people are freaking out.
Oh, no, SEO is dead.
Oh, no, I'm at the work scale where I have to prove to everybody that SEO is more important than ever.
Ah, right.
And so I've done plenty of presentations on how much more prevalent SEO is.
Matter of fact, so I've been consulting for a year on my own, under my own name, and one of my clients, actually, my old company, Known, reached out to me.
They're like, okay, so Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center is, we're working with them.
Their board of directors, all doctors, they're typing in cancer treatment.
They're like, why aren't we ranking for number one, right?
Oh, no, why don't we?
So it's really funny because MSK has a brilliant marketing team, has their usual struggles for hospital administration, and they couldn't get through to them.
So they hired a third party of me, and I went in and I presented at Google because that's their paid marketing.
And we both presented, and I led a discussion to essentially help them understand what's going on in the SEO world.
And just knowing my clients, knowing who these people were, it couldn't be, you know, talking to them.
It had to be.
The conversation I had to hear them through really to understand what their come from is and their frustrations and to just sort of appease them along the way.
At some point, they just needed to hear that things have changed where, you know, cancer treatment is pretty tough, but so they're ranking number three, right?
But anyway, long story short is, you know, to help create a strategy to help C-Suite understand what's happening in the SEO long game and the AI long game is something that I love to do.
I'm passionate about it.
And that's essentially what I did building out the SEO department over at Known.
Sure.
So that's, that's the start.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Amazing.
Yeah.
I mean, it's so interesting.
It's like people like keep thinking that they can get fancier than SEO and it's like you still want to rank like maybe like maybe you don't want to do that work anymore.
So that's so you need somebody to do it.

I mean visibility is a real thing and whether you're trying to rank for a keyword or rank through technical or rank through the relationships and partnerships from like backlinking like you still really need to understand like you know Google's eat and my money my life you know experience you know authoritativeness trustworthiness and I know I just skipped a E but you know there's it's more interesting than ever to align truth and behind like scaling truth.
I mean honestly.
honestly.

Behind content development and sort of like passing that along down the funnel is so important to map out strategy that is clear, concise, and doesn't have any fluff, as I feel like just so old school, but it still exists.

And so, you know, I've shared with you as an SEO director, but there's been so much general admin and working with partnering across departments and across clients and ensuring that everything is mapped out via calendar.
And, you know, I've certainly, you know, had to evangelize and educate.
And, and also understand people's come from to be a better manager.
Okay.
So that's where I'm going.
Let me jump into the questions, because I feel like we're getting into that, like, as you're talking, so might as well, like, give you, like, the, the prompt, and then you can riff from that.
Absolutely.
Okay, yeah.
So you were talking about, like, aligning your, aligning calendars, like, for multiple teams, so that everybody can kind of, like, know what's on the plan.
How do you think about juggling, like, deadlines across multiple accounts as well?

I think that it's, you have to understand the size of the team, and what perhaps is already on their plate, and managing, you know, is the team lean, too lean, or doesn't need to scale ahead, look for another person.
Um, but, uh.
You know, it all depends on the expectation, right?
Is it a two-week sprint, right?
Like, is there, like, scrum control?
And so, you know, working with, you know, SaaS products and such, it hasn't been an issue thus far, where, you know, people...

People have their own feelings about working across a schedule, but, you know, there are deadlines, you know, and as long as things are complete, and there's communication on expectation, then I'm usually pretty good and comfortable and confident.
Got it.
Okay, and then, can you give me an example?
Of a situation where you were responsible for driving a strategic initiative, but had to like define the path forward and all the steps involved yourself.
So how did you establish clarity, align others, kind of get cross-functional buy-in and everybody moving in the right direction?
Sure.
So Sesame Workshop, the parent not-for-profit for Sesame Street, is still a client of mine.
And they were also at Known.
And so when I started working with them, we redefined sesameworkshop.org and we migrated a bunch of other subdomain websites, military and communities, dot, et cetera, et cetera.
And so I worked across there, they hired a third-party technical...
Uh...

An engineering company, and essentially what we did was exactly what you were talking about pertaining to Calendar, is we developed how we saw each other, how we would work with one another when X would be developed, when Y would be developed, and we would just start to overlay and map out what that would look like.

And then, of course, a final meeting, we would make sure that everything made sense to partnership, and of course, that also just included the general big products like keyword research and competitor analysis, but really like on-page optimization for the new site was massive.
But yeah, like...
Coordinating calendars, essentially, making sure that there were no gaps there was important.
Yeah, yeah.
Okay, that sounds good.
Were there any challenges that came up that were unplanned, that you had to kind of work around or shift priorities through?
Yes.
So when we were developing our keyword research, one of our company partners were pretty involved.
They don't know anything about SEO.
And it was unfortunate because, but it was unfortunate that he was speaking in a place that made things a little bit more challenging where I needed to manage.
Where he, he had, there's, he had, what did he say?
Regardless of what he said, there were some projects that would have burned through time and expense that we didn't have time for.
Where him and I needed to have just a few side chats to ensure that we were both realigned because there are a few initial meetings with the Sesame Workshop that were less than lovely.
And, but we vetted that through.
And of course, you know, they're, they're happy enough with my work that we continue to partner.
Yeah.
All right.
And I should also mention that the end result with that partner was...
So known did a mass bunch of layoffs, they went from 700 to about 300.
And they had let go of the SEO department.
And this particular partner recommended for Sesame Street to continue on with me so that there would be no issues moving along.
So, you know, that issue that had occurred a few years ago, it was just water under the bridge.
Nice.
Yeah, that's it.
I'm sorry, I'm like, one of my cats is trying to jump up on the table.
So, okay.
All right.
Cool.
Okay.

So this is, you know, I think we do want to keep thinking about team progression, and making sure that the teams and the pods that are working directly with clients on a day-to-day basis feel ready and feel like they're learning.
So can you talk to me a little bit about your past management experience and, you know, as you, if you were setting a strategic initiative, how you divided work across teams specifically?
Sure.
So when I, when I was over at Known, I, the expectation when they brought me on was to have a team of four within the first year.

Um, unfortunately that didn't end up working out from budget cuts, but, um, what I did, we were, I was so closely aligned with our analytics team, um, our engineering team that it was pretty much, um, inherently known with other directors that, um, there were strategists on their team that, um, you know, I added things on, like we, we added things on.
On their schedule and, uh, work together to partner, um, internally.
Okay.
And I'm sorry, I had forgotten the question you had asked.
Yeah, so how do you think about like managing work across teams when you're setting strategy?
Thank you.
That is all I know.

And because a lot of the work that I did, all the work that I did at Known, I was both developing out the strategy and procuring it, both for whatever the project was, whatever the reporting was, whether it was weekly, monthly, quarterly, annual.
But for other teams, like before, Known and I worked at...

MRM, I did end up working and supporting as a manager with other strategists for, like, Benjamin Moore and ensuring that they had a clear, comfortable idea for how that strategy was preliminarily mapped out.

Um, uh, where we would have, uh, like, uh, uh, meetings, uh, I'm losing the word for it, but, um, kickoff meetings, um, and, um, to ensure that the, the launch, uh, aligned strategies that we developed out in-house and then polished off with our, uh, with our specific clients.
Awesome.
Um, you know, as you were managing teams or, or,
Coordinating across projects.
was the biggest mistake that you made working, you know, shepherding a project through to completion?
I think, I'm sure there's something really truly looming out there.

What really comes to mind is that relationship with that known partner, where I think what would have helped streamline our discussion and streamlined the, you know, SEO function with Sesame Street and other clients that I worked with him on, was just to make, to sit down with him and to really help him understand how my mind works, how I...
For him to help me understand how he thinks, how he likes to flesh things out, I think initially that was a learning experience where that could have been vetted earlier on.
But, I mean, at this point, you know, we've certainly more than mended things and found success from there.
Yeah.
So have you managed teams before?
From a, not directly, not directly.
Okay.
All good.
Okay.
Those are all my questions.
And just so you know, we're not, I'm not cutting this short.
These usually only take about 30 minutes because they only have three questions.
Okay.
My list, really.
What questions do you have for me?
Sure.
So what exactly, like you said, there's a few different kinds of natural leaders that you've brought on to the team as account directors.
You know, is there something specific that you're thinking about bringing on to the team now that you see really filling in the full circle, 360 degrees?
I think, like, more, like, technical SEO strategy is probably, like, is something that we have a couple of, but the clients that we're bringing on are continually very technical.
So, like, the technical SEO people are pretty strapped for time right now.
So I think that would be, that's something that we could probably use more of to fill out, to match up with the kind of clients we're bringing on.
Got it.
Okay.
Yeah, I'm certainly more, to speak into that, I work both technical and I work both content.
And when I was, it was almost the beginning of my career where, for SEO, I was brought into Omnicom, and then we lost a client.
It was GSK, GlaxoSmithKline.
I moved to another company to work for my manager, and we developed out an SEO department there.
The GO McIntyre.
And I'm just saying this in short and with brevity.
And then a few years there, I moved to MRM.
This was during the great migration during COVID, if you remember that, where people are like, I could move here for greater happiness.
And anyway, so the SEO team at MRM was stripped as a result.
And so me and my manager, Ken, ended up doing the same thing, developing the SEO department from scratch.
So I've had my hand in developing and redeveloping 360-degree strategy for SEO.
And I'm very comfortable with starting wherever it is needed, depending on client needs, and speaking to any particular.
Part of it.
You can test me if you'd like.
But, you know, and then, of course, with AI, it sounds like there are some, you know, it's an exciting time to be in SEO.
And, yeah, I'd be interested in testing methods and experimenting, whatever that looks like, whatever there's time for, and even focusing on the website, et cetera, et cetera.
Yeah, cool.
Though that question was more for you, I just like filling in the blanks.
So I know the CEO has developed GrowthX with a, like, a master plan, a master strategy, with how things, how strategies should unfold for clients, right?
There's a process.
And so I'm curious about how that process.
master plan, a master plan, plan,
Gets to be implemented across different clients.
And, you know, I know it changes, but how is that typically vetted across other clients?
Yeah, so, I mean, we focus really, like, on blog content and building out, like, organic search results.
And so that's the, like, lane that we operate most strongly in.
And so we have an implementation team that we call the strategy sprint.
So for eight weeks, every client that comes on board spends eight weeks with our kind of, like, our best team.
And they set kind of, like, they do, like, that initial.
Content audit, keyword identification, like, and gap analysis, things like that.

And then, you know, depending on the client, we're building out, we build out, we call them artifacts, but just like all of the contextual pieces that go into informing the tone and how they want to talk about the product offering, things like that.
And all of those inform the writing guidelines for our AI workflows.
So once we implement the AI workflow, we, you know, we can think a little bit more about, like, we can optimize for technicality or, like, the kind of product offering.

But we do some deep dives, like, with their, like, head of product or engineering team, sales team sometimes about, like, how people are using the product and talking about it and, like, really getting deep on what
What the product actually is.
So that's how we cater to the different industries, too, is we get really deep on the offering itself.
And when you do that, do you set up any sort of AI agents to support those sorts of guidelines?
Yeah, yeah, we do.
Okay, cool.
I assume there's a standardized approach first, and then it's sort of refined per requests or per what the client is looking for.
Sorry, can you say that again?
Sure.
So I assume that growthx supports a few different types of AI agents under its engine.
you, Jen.
Thank Hello, Thank

But when a client is looking for, when you're trying to launch something for a specific client, what does it look like to go back as the account director to work with the engineers for those AI agents?
Yeah, so we're not working with the engineering team directly, we have like client ops engineers who help who are like bashing bugs and stuff like that.
But we have templated pipelines that we're implementing.
And then there are kind of like Madlibs where they're pulling in the writing guidelines or the product context, the company focus, things like that.
And so the agent like the templates built, and then you just say reference this document reference that document.
Do this kind of research, like don't look at, you know, don't ever link to these competitors.
Like we set those rules and the codes already written.
And so we're not reinventing the wheel every time we're building.
A new pipeline.
Cool.
Exactly.
How do you like working at GrowthX?
I mean, I know it's a relatively new company.
What has it been like?
And how many people are on your team?
Um, so I think we have almost 50 people on the delivery team.
Um, like five directors.
Um, and yeah, it's been really good.
think, you know, there's lots of changes we've, we're still playing with what the right org structure is, and who does what job.
I think swim lanes are a little bit, um, fuzzy.
So we need, like, we want people who are really willing to, like, dive in, can do any piece.
of the work if they need to, if something gets dropped, things like that.
So it's been very, it's been wild so far, but people are so excited to work with us that it's, you know, like we get very positive feedback too.
That is a great entry into client relations.
They're mostly positive.
What are some of the challenges that your team has had with some, some of the clients and how have those been tweaked?
Yeah, I mean, we meet with clients weekly now.

think that that will probably like the engagement managers or account managers will probably move to be meeting with clients weekly or biweekly and directors will probably not be as involved on a day to day basis.
So the relationship management will really be to like the strategist slash account manager slash engage.
My manager title still changing.
Yeah.
Okay.
Are there any other questions for me in terms of anything?
No, no questions for me.
Okay.
What are the next steps?
So I think you'll hear from Tucker on what's next.
Usually there is one other person and then a panel.
So that's the normal process, but Tucker will let you know.
Perfect.
I appreciate your time, Andi.
Thank you.
Yeah.
Thanks, Ben.
Have a good one.
You too.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Interview Ben Kent for the Account Director role.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=296.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Role Evolution:** The Account Director role is shifting from dedicated pods to a pooled, ticketing model. This optimizes director time for high-value strategic work (e.g., SEO audits) by offloading daily client comms to Account Managers.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=330.0)
  - [**Candidate Fit:** Ben Kent's 10 years of SEO experience, including building departments from scratch (Known, MRM), directly addresses the company's urgent need for more technical SEO capacity to match its growing client base.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=1627.0)
  - [**Strategic Acumen:** Kent demonstrated the ability to lead complex projects (Sesame Workshop migration) and manage difficult stakeholders, turning a challenging partner relationship into a strong advocate.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=1016.0)
  - [**Company Context:** GrowthX is a 1-year-old company scaling rapidly (\~50 delivery staff). This growth creates a dynamic environment with evolving roles, requiring adaptable team members.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=2110.0)

## Topics

### The Evolving Account Director Role at GrowthX

  - [The company is evolving its structure to support two business models: a traditional services org and a new product-led org built around a proprietary AI content platform.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=330.0)
  - [**New Operating Model:**](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=330.0)
      - [**Pods:** Account Manager (daily comms, PM) + Managing Editor (AI platform ops).](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=400.0)
      - [**Directors:** Pooled resource for high-value strategic work (e.g., SEO audits, content strategy).](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=449.0)
      - [**Rationale:** Optimizes director time for strategic tasks that don't require weekly client meetings, while allowing for flexible director assignment based on client needs (e.g., technical vs. editorial focus).](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=449.0)

### Candidate Experience & Strategic Approach

  - [**SEO Philosophy:** Views generative AI as making SEO more critical than ever, not obsolete.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=581.0)
      - [**Example:** Educated Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center's board on SEO's long-game value to manage unrealistic expectations.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=638.0)
  - [**Strategic Initiative Example (Sesame Workshop):**](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=1016.0)
      - [Led the migration and redesign of `sesameworkshop.org`.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=1038.0)
      - [**Process:** Coordinated calendars and deliverables across internal teams and a third-party engineering firm.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=1016.0)
      - [**Challenge:** Managed a partner at Known who proposed unfeasible projects.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=1152.0)
          - [**Resolution:** Used side chats to realign on scope and budget.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=1152.0)
          - [**Outcome:** The partner later recommended Kent for continued work after Known's SEO department was laid off, demonstrating a successful relationship recovery.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=1253.0)
  - [**Management Experience:**](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=1305.0)
      - [Built SEO departments from scratch at Known and MRM.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=1699.0)
      - [No direct reports at Known due to budget cuts, but partnered closely with analytics and engineering teams.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=1320.0)
      - [Supported strategists at MRM on client kickoffs (e.g., Benjamin Moore).](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=1436.0)

### GrowthX's Client Strategy & AI Platform

  - [**Client Onboarding:** All new clients complete an 8-week "strategy sprint."](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=1838.0)
      - [**Goal:** Deep dives with client teams (product, engineering, sales) to create "artifacts" (e.g., tone guides) that inform AI workflow guidelines.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=1901.0)
  - [**AI Platform:** Uses templated pipelines with "Madlibs" for customization.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=2028.0)
      - [**Process:** Directors define rules (e.g., research sources, competitor exclusions) by referencing client-specific documents, not by writing code.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=2028.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Tucker:** Contact Ben Kent with next steps.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=2245.0)
  - [**Ben Kent:** Await contact from Tucker.](https://fathom.video/share/5Kb2-_WGCKgcUmvrwbA3nzTrS9ruyVgv?tab=summary&timestamp=2245.0)

action_items.description: Email Ben re: next steps (interview + panel)
